summary: Mechanically split large monolithic assembly ranges into more focused object files for the lb and ft systems. The PR moved the early fighter range out of the renamed ft code file into asm/melee/ft/fighter.s, split lbfile-sized code out of asm/melee/lb/code_8000E9F0.s, moved func_8000E9F0 into lbvector.s, created asm/melee/lb/code_80016A54.s for the later lb range, and updated obj_files.mk so the new objects link in order.
what_changed: Renamed asm/melee/ft/code_800679B0.s to asm/melee/ft/code_8006DBF4.s, then removed the 0x800679B0-0x8006DBF0 fighter range from that file.
Added asm/melee/ft/fighter.s containing the fighter.c-labelled range starting at func_800679B0 and covering .text 0x800679B0-0x8006DBF0.
Reduced asm/melee/lb/code_8000E9F0.s by removing earlier and later chunks, leaving it as the .text range 0x8000ED54-0x80016158.
Added asm/melee/lb/lbfile.s containing a new .text range 0x8001615C-0x80016A50.
Added asm/melee/lb/code_80016A54.s containing the later lb range 0x80016A54-0x80028B98.
Extended asm/melee/lb/lbvector.s so its section range is 0x8000D2EC-0x8000ED50 and includes func_8000E9F0, which was removed from code_8000E9F0.s.
Updated obj_files.mk to add lbfile.o and code_80016A54.o after lb/code_8000E9F0.o, and to replace ft/code_800679B0.o with ft/fighter.o followed by ft/code_8006DBF4.o.
smart_moves: Preserved link-order semantics by adding the new object files to obj_files.mk in address order rather than only renaming files.
Used address-based filenames for unknown or not-yet-named ranges, while giving known subsystem chunks meaningful names such as fighter.s and lbfile.s.
Kept the split mechanical: most changes are moves between assembly files with section address comments adjusted to the new boundaries.
Moved func_8000E9F0 into lbvector.s, indicating the split was based on logical module ownership rather than blindly carving only by file size.
decomp_lessons: When preparing large asm blobs for future C decompilation, split them into object-sized or module-sized ranges before attempting semantic rewrites.
After carving an assembly file, update both the .section address comments and obj_files.mk; matching depends on the new objects being linked in the original address order.
A named module file can coexist with address-named residual files: use semantic names when evidence exists, and address names for remaining unknown ranges.
Moving code between asm files should be treated as a build-graph change, not just a source organization change, because object ordering affects final DOL layout.
naming_conventions: Use subsystem paths for asm modules, e.g. asm/melee/ft and asm/melee/lb.
Use semantic module filenames when a known source/module name is available, e.g. fighter.s and lbfile.s.
Use code_<start-address>.s for unidentified residual assembly ranges, e.g. code_8006DBF4.s, code_8000E9F0.s, and code_80016A54.s.
Keep function labels as existing func_<address> names when no stronger symbol evidence is present.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Split monolithic assembly by contiguous address ranges and record each range in the .section comment.
Maintain object-file order in obj_files.mk to preserve final text layout.
For file splits, prefer moving exact asm blocks without editing instruction text so matching risk stays low.
Use boundary addresses in filenames and section comments to make future matching/debugging searchable.
follow_up_queries: Which later PR converts asm/melee/ft/fighter.s to C?
Which later PR names functions in asm/melee/lb/lbfile.s?
Which object boundary introduced code_80016A54.s into C decompilation?
Did later cleanup rename func_8000E9F0 after moving it into lbvector?
